steps.title: Identify the structure around the blank
steps.content: The sentence has a long introductory prepositional phrase: "In addition to having hard hooves that can dig into nearly any groove or ______." After the blank comes the main (independent) clause: "mountain goats have slender bodies ideal for scaling nearly ninety-degree cliffs." Standard English requires a comma after a long introductory phrase to separate it from the main clause.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — crack, or: Places a comma mid-phrase and then adds 'or,' which would try to continue the list after the comma — breaking the phrase apart and making the structure incoherent. ✗
  • Choice B — crack or: The word 'or' without a following comma makes "groove or crack or mountain goats" — running the introductory phrase directly into the main clause with no separating comma. ✗
  • Choice C — crack: Completes the phrase but provides no comma, so the introductory phrase runs directly into the main clause. ✗
  • Choice D — crack,: Completes the phrase as "groove or crack" and places the required comma before the main clause. Correct punctuation for a long introductory phrase. ✓
D is correct.
